📋 50/30/20 Budget Planner
💡 Money Rules to Live By
🎯 Pay yourself first — save BEFORE spending.
🧾 On-time payments build your credit score.
📱 Cancel subscriptions you forgot you have.
🔥 Emergency fund = 3 months of expenses.
💳 Never carry a credit card balance.
📊 What gets measured gets managed.
⛪ Honor God with the first 10%.
🏠 $50/week = $2,600/year toward your goals.
🧾
TAX EDUCATION
Understanding taxes is one of the highest-ROI skills you can have. Most people overpay or underpay because they never learned this. You will.
📅 CRITICAL TAX DATES
JAN 31
W-2s & 1099s due from employers
APR 15
Federal tax filing deadline
JUN 15
Q2 estimated tax payment (self-employed)
SEP 15
Q3 estimated tax payment
OCT 15
Extended filing deadline
JAN 15
Q4 estimated tax payment
📖 THE BASICS EVERYONE MUST KNOW
What Is Income Tax?

The federal government taxes your income on a progressive bracket system — you don't pay the same rate on every dollar. The first chunk is taxed at 10%, the next at 12%, and so on up to 37% for the highest earners. You only pay the higher rate on the dollars above each threshold.

Example: If you earn $45,000, you don't pay 22% on all of it. You pay 10% on the first $11,600, 12% on the next $35,550, and 22% on just the remaining $~1,500.
W-2 vs. 1099 — Know Which You Are
W-2 EMPLOYEE
Employer withholds taxes automatically. You get a W-2 form. File by April 15. Usually get a refund.
1099 SELF-EMPLOYED
No withholding. You owe 15.3% self-employment tax PLUS income tax. Must pay quarterly estimates.
Gross Pay vs. Net Pay vs. Taxable Income
💰 Gross Pay — Everything you earned before any deductions
minus pre-tax deductions (401k, health insurance, FSA)
📊 Taxable Income — What the IRS actually taxes
minus federal, state, FICA taxes
🏦 Net Pay (Take-Home) — What hits your bank account
✂️ DEDUCTIONS & CREDITS — FREE MONEY

Deductions reduce your taxable income. Credits reduce your actual tax bill. Credits are better — dollar for dollar.

Standard Deduction (2024)
Single: $14,600
Married Filing Jointly: $29,200
Head of Household: $21,900
Most people take the standard deduction. Only itemize if your deductible expenses exceed these amounts.
Common Deductions (if itemizing)
✓ Mortgage interest
✓ State & local taxes (up to $10k)
✓ Charitable donations
✓ Student loan interest
✓ Medical expenses (>7.5% AGI)
✓ Business expenses (self-employed)
Valuable Tax Credits
up to $7,830
Earned Income Tax Credit — For lower-income earners. Refundable — can exceed what you owe.
up to $2,500
American Opportunity Credit — First 4 years of college tuition.
up to $2,000
Lifetime Learning Credit — Any college or vocational training, any year.
up to $3,000
Child & Dependent Care Credit — Childcare while you work.
up to $7,500
EV Tax Credit — New electric vehicle purchases.
💼 SELF-EMPLOYED & SIDE HUSTLE TAXES

If you earn ANY income outside a W-2 job — gigs, freelance, reselling, Venmo payments — the IRS considers you self-employed. This section is critical.

The Self-Employment Tax Hit
15.3% self-employment tax on top of your income tax. This covers both halves of Social Security (6.2%) and Medicare (1.45%) that employers normally pay for W-2 workers. You pay both sides.
The 25–30% Rule

Set aside 25–30% of every self-employment payment into a separate savings account the moment it hits. Don't touch it. This covers federal income tax + self-employment tax. Many freelancers get blindsided at tax time.

Quarterly Estimated Taxes

If you expect to owe more than $1,000 in taxes, the IRS requires you to pay quarterly. Miss them and you'll owe a penalty.

Q1 income
Due Apr 15
Q2 income
Due Jun 15
Q3 income
Due Sep 15
Q4 income
Due Jan 15
Business Deductions — Reduce What You Owe
Track and deduct legitimate business expenses:
📱 Phone (business %) · 💻 Computer/equipment · 🚗 Mileage (67¢/mile in 2024) · 🏠 Home office (sq ft %) · 📚 Education/courses · ✈️ Business travel · 🛠 Tools & supplies · 📣 Marketing/advertising
Keep receipts for EVERYTHING. A $1,000 business expense at 22% bracket = $220 back in your pocket.
🏦 RETIREMENT ACCOUNTS = TAX STRATEGY
Traditional 401k / IRA — Tax Now or Later?
TRADITIONAL 401k/IRA
Contributions reduce taxable income now. Pay taxes when you withdraw in retirement. Better if you're in a high tax bracket today.
ROTH 401k/IRA
Pay taxes now, withdraw tax-FREE in retirement. Usually better if you're young and expect to be in a higher bracket later.
2024 limits: 401k = $23,000 · IRA = $7,000. These contributions compound TAX-FREE for decades.
HSA — The Triple Tax Advantage

Health Savings Account: contributions are pre-tax, growth is tax-free, withdrawals for medical are tax-free. Only available with high-deductible health plans. 2024 limit: $4,150 (individual), $8,300 (family).

🧮 QUICK TAX ESTIMATOR

Estimate your federal income tax and self-employment tax.

Federal Income Tax
$0
Self-Emp. Tax
$0
Effective Rate
0%
Est. Total Owed
$0
🔗 FREE FILING RESOURCES
🏛 IRS Free File — Free if income under $79,000 📊 TurboTax Free Edition 💼 H&R Block Free Online ✅ FreeTaxUSA — $0 federal always 💳 IRS Direct Pay — Pay what you owe 💼 IRS Self-Employed Tax Center 📝 W-4 Withholding Calculator
⚡ PRO TAX MOVES MOST PEOPLE NEVER LEARN
💡 Adjust your W-4 to get close to $0 refund/$0 bill. A big refund means you gave the IRS an interest-free loan all year.
💡 Max your 401k first — every dollar contributed reduces your taxable income dollar for dollar this year.
💡 Track mileage if you drive for work, deliveries, or your business. At 67¢/mile, 5,000 miles = $3,350 deduction.
💡 Hire a CPA if you're self-employed — they typically save 3–5x their cost in found deductions and avoided mistakes.
💡 Never ignore IRS mail. It won't go away. Most letters are solvable — respond within 30 days.
💡 Keep records 7 years. The IRS can audit up to 6 years back for substantial underreporting.

⚠️ UPCOMING DEADLINES
📝 EXAM TRACKER
📝 SAT — Scholastic Assessment Test
FORMAT
⏱ 2 hrs 14 min total
📖 Reading & Writing: 54 Q, 64 min
🔢 Math: 44 Q, 70 min
📱 Digital adaptive format
✅ Score range: 400–1600
SCORE TARGETS
🏅 Good: 1050+
⭐ Competitive: 1200+
🌟 Excellent: 1400+
🎯 National avg: ~1010
🏆 Top schools: 1500+
WHEN TO TAKE
📅 Best: Spring Junior year
🔄 Retake: Fall Senior year
🗓 Dates: Mar, May, Jun, Aug, Oct, Nov
💰 Fee: ~$60 (waivers available)
📝 Register: collegeboard.org
🔗 FREE OFFICIAL RESOURCES
📚
Khan Academy Official SAT Prep FREE
Personalized practice linked to your PSAT scores. The #1 free resource.
 🎯
Official CollegeBoard Practice Tests FREE
4 full-length digital practice tests with official answer explanations.
 💻
Bluebook App (Official Test Platform) FREE
Practice on the exact same app used on test day.
💡 Pro tip: Connect Khan Academy to your College Board account for a custom study plan based on your actual PSAT results. Students who study 20+ hours on Khan Academy average a 115-point score increase.
📊 ACT — American College Testing
FORMAT
⏱ 2 hrs 55 min (+ optional Writing)
📖 English: 75 Q, 45 min
🔢 Math: 60 Q, 60 min
🔬 Reading: 40 Q, 35 min
🧪 Science: 40 Q, 35 min
✅ Score: 1–36 composite
SCORE TARGETS
🏅 Good: 21+
⭐ Competitive: 25+
🌟 Excellent: 30+
🎯 National avg: ~20.9
🏆 Top schools: 34+
SAT vs ACT
🧪 ACT has a Science section
⏱ ACT is faster-paced per Q
🔢 SAT Math has more depth
📖 SAT Reading is more inference
✅ Take both — submit your best score
🔗 FREE OFFICIAL RESOURCES
🎯
Official ACT Free Test Prep FREE
Practice tests, question of the day, and official prep guides from ACT.
 📝
CrackACT — Real Past Tests FREE
Real past ACT exams with answer keys. Best timed practice available.
 📚
Khan Academy — ACT Science & Math FREE
Targeted practice for ACT Math and Science sections.
💡 Pro tip: Take a full timed practice SAT and ACT before deciding which to focus on. Most students perform 2–4 points better on one vs. the other.
🎖 ASVAB — Armed Services Vocational Aptitude Battery
9 SUBTESTS
🔤 General Science (GS)
➗ Arithmetic Reasoning (AR)
📖 Word Knowledge (WK)
📝 Paragraph Comprehension (PC)
🔢 Math Knowledge (MK)
⚡ Electronics Info (EI)
🔧 Auto & Shop Info (AS)
🔭 Mechanical Comprehension (MC)
💻 Assembling Objects (AO)
AFQT SCORE (Enlistment)
🪖 Army: 31+ AFQT
⚓ Navy: 35+
🦅 Marines: 32+
✈️ Air Force: 36+
🌊 Coast Guard: 40+
🌟 Elite jobs: 85+
KEY FACTS
⏱ ~3 hours total
💻 CAT-ASVAB: Computer adaptive
🔄 Retake: 30 days after 1st, then monthly
🎯 Focus on: AR, WK, MK, PC first
💰 Free through your recruiter
🔗 FREE STUDY RESOURCES
🎯
Official ASVAB Sample Questions FREE
Official sample questions for all 9 subtests from the test makers.
 🪖
March2Success (Army Sponsored) FREE
Free ASVAB study program sponsored by the U.S. Army.
 📝
Union Test Prep — ASVAB FREE
Free practice tests for every subtest with detailed explanations.
💡 Pro tip: Your AFQT score (AR + WK + MK + PC) determines if you qualify. Focus 70% of prep on those four subtests. Higher line scores open better MOS job options — aim for 60+ if you want choices.
🏆 AP Exams — Advanced Placement
HOW IT WORKS
📅 Exams in May each year
🎯 Scores: 1–5
✅ Score 3+ = college credit at most schools
💰 Fee: ~$98/exam (waivers available)
📝 38 subjects available
HIGHEST PASS RATES
🖥️ Computer Science Principles
🌍 Human Geography
🧬 Environmental Science
🏛️ US Gov & Politics
📊 Statistics
🎨 Art & Design
HARDEST EXAMS
🧪 AP Chemistry
⚛️ AP Physics C
📐 AP Calculus BC
🇺🇸 AP US History
🌐 AP World History
🗣️ AP Language & Composition
🔗 FREE STUDY RESOURCES
🎯
AP Classroom (Official) FREE with class
Official practice questions, progress checks, and past FRQs from CollegeBoard.
 📚
Khan Academy — AP Subject Content FREE
Full courses for AP Calc, Stats, Bio, Chemistry, Physics, and more.
 💡
Albert.io — AP Practice Free + paid
1,500+ free AP practice questions with detailed explanations across 20+ subjects.
💡 Pro tip: Even a 3 on an AP exam can save $1,000–$5,000 in college tuition per course. Always take the exam — even if you're unsure you'll pass.
🎯 PSAT — Preliminary SAT / National Merit
FORMAT
⏱ 2 hrs 13 min
📖 Reading & Writing: 54 Q
🔢 Math: 44 Q
✅ Score range: 320–1520
📅 Taken: October, 10th or 11th grade
💰 Fee: ~$18 (many schools cover it)
NATIONAL MERIT
🏆 Top ~50,000 scorers = Commended
🌟 Top ~16,000 = Semifinalist
💰 Finalists can win $2,500+ scholarships
🎓 Many colleges give full rides to NM Finalists
WHY IT MATTERS
🎯 Identifies SAT weak areas early
💡 Auto-links to Khan Academy prep
🏆 National Merit Scholarship path
✅ No pressure — doesn't count for admissions
📚
Khan Academy SAT/PSAT Prep FREE
Connect your College Board account for a personalized PSAT-based study plan.
 🎯
Official PSAT Practice Tests FREE
Full-length practice tests from CollegeBoard with answer explanations.
📜 GED — General Educational Development
4 SUBJECTS
🔢 Mathematical Reasoning (~115 min)
🔬 Science (~90 min)
🗳️ Social Studies (~70 min)
📖 Reasoning Through Language Arts (~150 min)
✅ Each scored 100–200 | Passing: 145+
SCORE LEVELS
⚪ Below Passing: under 145
✅ Pass: 145–164
🎓 GED College Ready: 165–174
🌟 College Ready + Credit: 175+
💰 Fee: ~$30/subject (~$120 total)
WHO IT'S FOR
🏠 Homeschool graduates
📋 High school dropouts
🔄 Adults returning to education
💼 Career advancement
🪖 Military enlistment eligibility
🎯
GED.com Official Study Guide FREE + paid
Official free study guide, practice tests, and the GED Ready practice exam.
 📚
Khan Academy — GED Prep FREE
Full GED prep courses for all 4 subjects with practice exercises.
🎓 CLEP — College Level Examination Program
WHAT IT IS
📜 Earn college credits by passing an exam
🎯 34 subject exams available
⏱ 90 minutes each
💰 $93/exam vs $1,000–$5,000/college course
✅ Score 50+ = passing at most schools
🎓 Accepted by 2,900+ colleges
POPULAR EXAMS
📚 English Composition
📊 College Algebra
🌍 Western Civilization I & II
🧬 Biology
💼 Intro to Business
🧠 Introductory Psychology
🌐 Spanish Language
MILITARY BENEFIT
🪖 Active duty military take CLEP FREE
🎯 Can complete entire associate degree
🏆 Many earn bachelor's degrees while serving
🔗 dantes.doded.mil for military access
🎯
Official CLEP Exam Guide FREE
Official exam descriptions, sample questions, and study materials for all 34 exams.
 🌟
Modern States — Free CLEP Courses + Voucher FREE
Complete free courses for 30+ CLEP exams and get a free exam voucher ($93 value).
💡 Pro tip: Modern States gives you free prep AND a free exam voucher. That's a potential $93 savings — and each exam can replace a $3,000+ college course.
🧠 Evidence-Based Study Techniques
⏱ Pomodoro Technique
25 min focused work → 5 min break → repeat 4x → 20 min long break. Use the Study Timer tab. Prevents burnout and keeps focus sharp.
🔁 Spaced Repetition
Review material at increasing intervals (1 day → 3 days → 1 week → 2 weeks). How Anki flashcards work. Dramatically beats cramming for long-term retention.
🗣️ Active Recall
Close the book and try to recall what you just read. Quiz yourself — don't re-read. Flashcards, practice problems, and teaching it to someone else all trigger active recall.
🌊 The Feynman Technique
1) Study the concept. 2) Explain it like you're teaching a 12-year-old. 3) Identify gaps. 4) Go back and fill them. If you can't explain it simply, you don't know it yet.
🎯 Interleaved Practice
Mix topics in the same session (Math → Science → History → Math) instead of studying one subject for hours. Feels harder but leads to 43% better test retention.
📅 Test Day Checklist
✅ Sleep 8+ hours the night before
✅ Eat a solid breakfast (protein + carbs)
✅ Arrive 30 min early
✅ Bring valid ID + admission ticket
✅ Approved calculator (SAT/ACT/GED)
✅ #2 pencils + extra eraser
✅ Water bottle + snack for break
✅ No cramming the morning of
🔗 Best Free Study Platforms
📚
Khan Academy 100% FREE
Math, science, history, SAT, ASVAB, AP — the GOAT of free education.
 🃏
Anki Flashcards FREE (desktop + web)
Spaced repetition flashcard system used by medical students worldwide.
 🧩
Quizlet Free + paid
Flashcards, matching, and test mode. Millions of pre-made sets for every subject.
 🧮
Wolfram Alpha FREE
Solve math, science, and engineering problems step by step. Essential for STEM.
 🌐
Coursera — Free Audit FREE to audit
Thousands of college-level courses from top universities, free to audit.

💪
Health & Fitness
?HOW TO USE
Weight: Log weight + goal → see progress chart with dots labeled by date. Nutrition: Log meals → daily macro totals update live. Habits: Same as dashboard check-ins — synced.
🥗 Why Nutrition Matters — Especially Now
Your body is still building itself. Your brain won't fully develop until age 25. Your bones are reaching peak density. Your hormones are establishing patterns that will last decades. What you eat right now literally determines the body and brain you'll have for the rest of your life. This isn't about dieting or being skinny — it's about giving your body the materials it needs to build the strongest, healthiest version of you.
🔬 THE MACRONUTRIENTS — Your Body's Fuel
🥩
Protein
What it does: Builds and repairs every cell in your body — muscles, skin, hair, organs, immune system, hormones, and enzymes. Without enough protein, your body literally cannot grow or heal properly.

How much: 0.8–1g per pound of body weight if active. A 150lb teen needs ~120–150g/day. Most teens get less than half this.

Best sources: Chicken breast, eggs, Greek yogurt, fish, lean beef, turkey, cottage cheese, beans, lentils, tofu, protein shakes.

The truth: Protein is the single most important macronutrient for a growing body. If you only fix one thing about your diet, eat more protein.
🍞
Carbohydrates
What it does: Your brain and muscles' primary fuel source. Carbs are not the enemy — they are literally what powers your thinking and movement. The TYPE of carb matters.

Good carbs (complex): Oatmeal, brown rice, sweet potatoes, whole wheat bread, fruits, vegetables, quinoa. These digest slowly, giving sustained energy. Eat these.

Bad carbs (simple/refined): White bread, candy, soda, pastries, chips, sugary cereal. These spike blood sugar then crash, leaving you tired and hungry. Limit these.

How much: 45–65% of total calories. An active teen needs 250–400g/day. Prioritize complex carbs with fiber.
🥑
Fats
What it does: Builds brain cells (your brain is 60% fat), produces hormones, absorbs vitamins A/D/E/K, protects organs, and provides long-lasting energy. Fat does NOT make you fat — excess calories make you fat.

Healthy fats: Avocado, olive oil, nuts (almonds, walnuts), seeds, salmon, eggs, dark chocolate. These reduce inflammation and support brain development.

Unhealthy fats: Trans fats (fried fast food, margarine, packaged snacks). These increase inflammation, clog arteries, and damage heart health. Read labels — avoid "partially hydrogenated oils."

How much: 25–35% of total calories. Don't fear fat — just choose the right kinds.
🌾
Fiber
What it does: Feeds your gut bacteria (your "second brain"), regulates digestion, stabilizes blood sugar, lowers cholesterol, and keeps you feeling full. Most teens get less than half the recommended amount.

Best sources: Beans, lentils, oats, vegetables, fruits with skin, whole grains, broccoli, berries, chia seeds, sweet potatoes.

How much: 25–35g/day. The average teen gets 10–12g. Increase gradually (too fast causes bloating). Drink extra water with fiber.
💧 Water — The Most Underrated Nutrient
Your body is 60% water. Your brain is 75% water. Even 2% dehydration reduces mental performance by 20% and physical performance by 10%. Most teens are chronically dehydrated without knowing it — headaches, fatigue, trouble concentrating, dry skin, and irritability are all dehydration symptoms.

How much: Minimum 64 oz (8 cups) per day. If you're active or it's hot: 80–120 oz. Your urine should be pale yellow — dark yellow means you're dehydrated.

What counts: Water, sparkling water, herbal tea. What doesn't: Soda, energy drinks, and juice are mostly sugar with water — they don't hydrate the same way. Coffee and caffeinated drinks are mild diuretics.

Pro tip: Carry a water bottle everywhere. Drink a full glass when you wake up. Set reminders until it becomes habit. This single change improves energy, skin, focus, and mood.
🍬 Sugar — The Legal Drug in Your Food
The average American teen consumes 73 grams of added sugar per day — nearly 3x the recommended limit. Sugar is added to almost everything: bread, pasta sauce, yogurt, granola bars, "healthy" smoothies, sports drinks.

What excess sugar does: Spikes insulin → energy crash → cravings → repeat cycle. Over time: weight gain, type 2 diabetes, acne, tooth decay, inflammation, fatty liver disease, increased anxiety and depression. Sugar activates the same brain reward pathways as addictive substances.

The limit: American Heart Association recommends no more than 25g (6 tsp) of added sugar per day for teens. One can of Coke has 39g. One Starbucks Frappuccino has 50g+.

How to reduce: Read nutrition labels — look for "Added Sugars." Drink water instead of soda/juice. Choose whole fruits over fruit snacks. Swap sugary cereal for oatmeal with berries. You don't have to eliminate sugar — just become aware of how much you're consuming.
🌿 Gluten, Dairy & Food Sensitivities
Gluten is a protein in wheat, barley, and rye. For people with celiac disease (~1% of the population), gluten causes serious intestinal damage. For others with gluten sensitivity, it can cause bloating, fatigue, and brain fog. For most people, gluten is perfectly fine — you don't need to avoid it unless you have symptoms.

Dairy — roughly 65% of humans are lactose intolerant to some degree. If milk, cheese, or ice cream causes bloating, gas, or stomach pain, you may be one of them. Lactose-free milk, hard cheeses, and yogurt (where bacteria have broken down the lactose) are alternatives. Calcium can come from broccoli, almonds, fortified plant milks, and sardines.

How to know: If you suspect a food sensitivity, try eliminating that food for 2–3 weeks, then reintroduce it and notice how you feel. A doctor can test for celiac disease and true allergies. Don't self-diagnose based on TikTok trends — get real information.
⚠️ What Bad Nutrition Does Over Time
The diseases that kill most Americans — heart disease, type 2 diabetes, many cancers, stroke — are overwhelmingly lifestyle diseases caused by decades of poor nutrition. They don't appear overnight. They build silently for 20–30 years.

Heart disease — the #1 killer in America. Begins with arterial damage from chronic inflammation, high blood sugar, and oxidized fats. The process starts in your teens and twenties.

Type 2 diabetes — once rare in young people, now diagnosed in teenagers at record rates. Caused by years of excess sugar and refined carbs overwhelming your insulin system.

Obesity — affects 42% of American adults and 20% of teens. It's not about willpower — it's about food systems designed to make you overeat. Understanding nutrition is the defense.

Mental health — your gut produces 90% of your serotonin (the "happiness" chemical). A diet high in processed food and sugar is directly linked to higher rates of depression and anxiety. Fixing your diet often improves mental health faster than any other single intervention.

The good news: You are young. Your body responds to good nutrition faster than at any other point in your life. The habits you build now will protect you for decades. You don't need to be perfect — you need to be intentional.
✅ The Simple Rules That Cover 90% of Good Nutrition
1. Eat protein at every meal (palm-sized portion minimum).
2. Eat vegetables or fruit at every meal (fill half your plate).
3. Drink water as your primary beverage (64+ oz/day).
4. Choose whole grains over refined (brown rice > white rice, whole wheat > white bread).
5. Limit added sugar to under 25g/day (read labels).
6. Eat real food most of the time (if your grandmother wouldn't recognize it as food, it probably isn't).
7. Don't skip meals — especially breakfast. Your brain needs fuel to learn.
8. Cook when you can. The single best thing you can do for your nutrition is learn to make 5 simple meals yourself.

You don't need supplements, superfoods, or a perfect diet. You need consistency with the basics. Eat real food, drink water, get enough protein, and limit junk. That puts you ahead of 90% of the population.

🔗 JOB BOARDS — Quick Links
Indeed LinkedIn Glassdoor ZipRecruiter Monster CareerBuilder Simply Hired 🏛 USA Jobs 🚀 Wellfound 🌐 Remote OK
➕ LOG AN APPLICATION
📋 MY APPLICATIONS
👔
WHAT TO WEAR
🕐
ARRIVE EARLY
🧠
STAR METHOD
🏆
STAND OUT
👔 WHAT TO WEAR
1
Research the company culture first. Tech startup = business casual. Law firm = full suit. Check their LinkedIn photos and Glassdoor reviews.
2
Always dress one level above what employees wear. If they wear jeans, wear slacks. If they wear slacks, wear a suit.
3
Fit is everything. A well-fitted $40 outfit beats a baggy $400 suit. Iron everything. No wrinkles.
4
Keep it simple. Conservative colors — navy, gray, black, white. Minimal jewelry. No heavy cologne/perfume.
5
Clean shoes matter more than people think. Interviewers notice. Polished, closed-toe shoes for professional settings.
★
Pro move: Lay out your entire outfit the night before — shirt, pants, belt, shoes, socks. Zero scrambling on the day.
💬 WHAT TO SAY
1
"Tell me about yourself" — Prepare a 90-second pitch: who you are, key experience, why you want THIS job. Practice it out loud until it flows naturally.
2
Use numbers whenever possible. "Increased sales 22%" beats "helped improve sales." Quantify everything you can.
3
Research the company deeply. Know their mission, recent news, products, competitors. Reference specifics: "I saw you just launched X — I'd love to contribute to..."
4
Ask smart questions. "What does success look like in this role after 90 days?" "What are the biggest challenges the team faces?" Shows you're thinking about impact, not just getting a job.
5
Send a thank-you email within 2 hours. 70% of candidates don't. Reference something specific from the conversation. This alone sets you apart.
★
Closing statement: "I want to be direct — I'm very excited about this role and this team. If there's anything else I can provide, I'd love to do that." Confidence closes.
🚫 WHAT NOT TO SAY
✕
"My weakness is I work too hard." Interviewers see through this instantly. Give a real weakness and what you're doing about it.
✕
Badmouthing your previous employer. Even if they were terrible. It signals you'll do the same to this company. Say: "I'm looking for a better cultural fit / more growth."
✕
"What does your company do?" — Failure to research is an instant disqualifier. Know their business before you walk in the door.
✕
Bringing up salary first. Let them mention it or wait until you have an offer. Asking too early signals you're only in it for money.
✕
Rambling answers. If you don't know, say "That's a great question — let me think for a second." Silence is better than word salad.
✕
Checking your phone. Turn it completely off. Not silent — off. Or leave it in your car. No exceptions.
🕐 TIMING & LOGISTICS
★
Arrive 10–15 minutes early. Not 30 minutes — that's awkward and burdens the receptionist. Not on time — "on time is late" in interviews.
1
Do a dry run the day before. Drive or commute the route at the same time of day. Know exactly where parking is and how far the walk is.
2
If you're going to be late, call immediately. Traffic happens. Call as soon as you know — not when you're 2 minutes away. It shows professionalism.
3
Treat everyone like they're the hiring manager. Receptionists, security guards, people in the elevator. They are asked for their impressions. Be warm to everyone.
4
Bring 5 printed copies of your resume. Even if they have it digitally. It shows preparedness and gives panel interviewers something to reference.
5
Also bring a portfolio or work samples if relevant — design work, presentations, reports, metrics you've driven. Physical proof beats claims.
🧠 THE STAR METHOD

Use this for every behavioral question ("Tell me about a time when..."). It structures your answer perfectly.

S
Situation — Set the scene briefly. What was the context? What challenge or opportunity existed?
T
Task — What was YOUR specific responsibility? What were you expected to accomplish?
A
Action — Exactly what did YOU do? Be specific. Focus on your individual contribution, not the team's.
R
Result — What was the measurable outcome? Use numbers. What did you learn?
★
Prepare 6–8 STAR stories before any interview. Most questions can be answered with a good story from your bank. Practice them until they feel natural, not memorized.
❓ BEHAVIORAL QUESTIONS BANK

Prepare answers to these before every interview. They're asked in 90% of professional interviews.

⚡ POWER TIPS — BE UNFORGETTABLE
1
Send a handwritten thank-you note. In addition to the email. Almost no one does this. It lands on their desk and stays there. Guaranteed to be remembered.
2
Connect on LinkedIn immediately after. Reference the interview. Interviewers check who looked them up — it signals genuine interest.
3
Prepare a "30-60-90 day plan" for senior roles. A one-page document showing what you'd accomplish in your first 90 days. Barely anyone does this. It's a conversation-stopper.
4
Mirror their language. If they say "impact," use that word back. If they say "move fast," reflect it. People hire people who sound like them.
5
Show energy and enthusiasm. Competence gets you in the room. Energy gets you the offer. Interviewers want someone who WANTS to be there. Let it show authentically.
6
Negotiate the offer. 85% of employers have room to move. Always counter with a specific number — "Based on my research and experience, I was targeting $X." The worst they say is no.
🎤 ELEVATOR PITCH BUILDER

Your pitch is a 30-60 second answer to "Tell me about yourself." Practice it until it flows naturally without sounding memorized.

FORMULA
1. Who you are — name + current role/status
2. What you do — key skills, experience, or focus area
3. What makes you unique — your differentiator or accomplishment
4. What you want — what you are looking for next
0 words
🎭 MOCK INTERVIEW LOG

Practice makes perfect. Log every mock interview — with a friend, family member, career counselor, or even in a mirror.

📋 PRACTICE HISTORY

School Resources
?HOW TO USE
Reference guides, practice tools, and calculators for school. Use the Math tab for formulas and practice, Science for measurements and methods, Calculators for quick math, Writing for essays, and Computer for tech skills.
➕ Arithmetic Basics

Order of Operations — PEMDAS:

Parentheses → Exponents → Multiplication & Division (left to right) → Addition & Subtraction (left to right)

Example: 3 + 4 × 2 = 3 + 8 = 11 (not 14)

Fractions:

Adding: Find common denominator → ½ + ⅓ = 3/6 + 2/6 = 5/6

Multiplying: Straight across → ½ × ⅓ = 1/6

Dividing: Flip second & multiply → ½ ÷ ⅓ = ½ × 3/1 = 3/2

Decimals ↔ Fractions ↔ Percentages:

0.5 = ½ = 50% | 0.25 = ¼ = 25% | 0.75 = ¾ = 75% | 0.1 = 1/10 = 10% | 0.33 = ⅓ ≈ 33%

📊 Percentages & Ratios

Finding a percentage: Part ÷ Whole × 100

Got 42 out of 50? → 42 ÷ 50 × 100 = 84%

Finding the amount from %: Whole × (% ÷ 100)

20% tip on $45? → 45 × 0.20 = $9.00

Percent change: (New - Old) ÷ Old × 100

Price went from $80 to $100? → (100-80) ÷ 80 × 100 = 25% increase

Ratios: A comparison. 3:5 means "3 for every 5." If ratio is 2:3 in a class of 50 → 2/(2+3) × 50 = 20 and 3/(2+3) × 50 = 30

📐 Geometry Essentials

Area formulas:

Rectangle: length × width | Triangle: ½ × base × height

Circle: π × r² (π ≈ 3.14159) | Trapezoid: ½(a+b) × h

Perimeter:

Rectangle: 2(l+w) | Circle (circumference): 2πr or πd

Volume:

Box: l × w × h | Cylinder: π × r² × h | Sphere: 4/3 × π × r³

Pythagorean Theorem: a² + b² = c² (right triangles only). If sides are 3 and 4, hypotenuse = √(9+16) = √25 = 5

🔤 Algebra Basics

Solving for x: Do the same thing to both sides.

2x + 5 = 13 → subtract 5 → 2x = 8 → divide by 2 → x = 4

Slope of a line: m = (y₂ - y₁) ÷ (x₂ - x₁)

Slope-intercept form: y = mx + b (m = slope, b = y-intercept)

Quadratic formula: x = (-b ± √(b²-4ac)) ÷ 2a

For ax² + bx + c = 0

FOIL method: (a+b)(c+d) = ac + ad + bc + bd

(x+3)(x+2) = x² + 2x + 3x + 6 = x² + 5x + 6

✏️ Quick Math Practice
✅ 0 correct ❌ 0 wrong 🔥 0 streak
📊 Multiplication Table
📏 Measurements & Conversions

Length:

1 inch = 2.54 cm | 1 foot = 12 in = 30.48 cm | 1 yard = 3 ft | 1 mile = 5,280 ft = 1.609 km | 1 meter = 3.28 ft | 1 km = 0.621 miles

Weight:

1 pound = 16 oz = 453.6 g | 1 kg = 2.205 lbs | 1 ton = 2,000 lbs

Volume:

1 cup = 8 fl oz | 1 pint = 2 cups | 1 quart = 2 pints | 1 gallon = 4 quarts = 3.785 L | 1 liter = 1.057 quarts

Temperature:

°F to °C: (°F - 32) × 5/9 | °C to °F: (°C × 9/5) + 32

Water freezes: 32°F / 0°C | Water boils: 212°F / 100°C | Body temp: 98.6°F / 37°C

🔬 The Scientific Method

1. Question — What do you want to know?

2. Research — What is already known?

3. Hypothesis — Your educated prediction (If... then...)

4. Experiment — Test it. Change ONE variable, keep everything else the same (controlled experiment).

5. Analyze — Collect data, look for patterns. Use tables and graphs.

6. Conclusion — Was your hypothesis supported? Why or why not?

Key terms: Variable (what changes), Control (what stays the same), Independent variable (what YOU change), Dependent variable (what you measure), Sample size (more = better data)

⚛️ Basic Chemistry & Physics

Matter: Solid → Liquid → Gas (add energy to go right, remove to go left)

Atoms: Protons (+) and Neutrons (neutral) in nucleus, Electrons (-) orbit outside

Atomic number = number of protons (defines the element)

Common elements: H (Hydrogen, 1), C (Carbon, 6), N (Nitrogen, 7), O (Oxygen, 8), Na (Sodium, 11), Fe (Iron, 26), Au (Gold, 79)

Newton's Laws:

1st: Objects at rest stay at rest unless acted on by a force

2nd: F = ma (Force = mass × acceleration)

3rd: Every action has an equal and opposite reaction

Speed = Distance ÷ Time | Density = Mass ÷ Volume

🌍 Earth & Space Basics

Layers of Earth: Crust (5-70km) → Mantle (2,900km) → Outer Core (liquid iron) → Inner Core (solid iron, 5,400°C)

Atmosphere layers: Troposphere (weather) → Stratosphere (ozone) → Mesosphere → Thermosphere → Exosphere

Solar System order: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune (My Very Educated Mother Just Served Us Nachos)

Moon phases: New → Waxing Crescent → First Quarter → Waxing Gibbous → Full → Waning Gibbous → Last Quarter → Waning Crescent → New

📊 Percentage Calculator
What is X% of Y?
% of
Grade Calculator: Score ÷ Total
out of
💰 Tip Calculator
Bill: $
🌡️ Unit Converter
📝 Essay Structure

5-Paragraph Essay (the foundation):

1. Introduction: Hook (grab attention) → Background (context) → Thesis statement (your main argument in one sentence — the most important sentence in the essay).

2-4. Body paragraphs: Each starts with a topic sentence (mini-thesis for that paragraph) → Evidence/examples → Analysis (explain WHY the evidence supports your point) → Transition to next point.

5. Conclusion: Restate thesis (different words) → Summarize key points → End with a broader insight or call to action. Never introduce new information in the conclusion.

Common mistakes: Starting with "In this essay I will..." (weak). Using "I think" or "I believe" (just state it). Not connecting evidence to argument. Conclusion that just repeats the intro word for word.

✅ Grammar Quick Reference

Their / There / They're:

Their = possession (their car). There = location (over there). They're = they are.

Your / You're:

Your = possession (your book). You're = you are.

Its / It's:

Its = possession (the dog wagged its tail). It's = it is.

Affect / Effect:

Affect = verb (it affects me). Effect = noun (the effect was big).

Then / Than:

Then = time/sequence (first this, then that). Than = comparison (bigger than).

Semicolons: Connect two related complete sentences. "I studied hard; the test was still difficult."

📚 Citation Basics (MLA & APA)

MLA (English/Humanities):

In-text: (Author Last Name Page#) → (Smith 42)

Works Cited: Last, First. "Title." Source, Publisher, Year.

APA (Science/Social Science):

In-text: (Author, Year) → (Smith, 2024)

References: Last, F. (Year). Title. Journal, Vol(Issue), Pages.

Plagiarism: Using someone else's words or ideas without credit. Even paraphrasing needs a citation. When in doubt, cite it. Tools: EasyBib.com, CitationMachine.net, Purdue OWL (the bible of citation help).

⌨️ Keyboard Shortcuts Everyone Should Know

Ctrl+C / Cmd+C — Copy | Ctrl+V / Cmd+V — Paste

Ctrl+Z — Undo (your best friend) | Ctrl+Y — Redo

Ctrl+A — Select all | Ctrl+S — Save (do this constantly)

Ctrl+F — Find (search text on any page or document)

Ctrl+P — Print | Ctrl+N — New document/window

Alt+Tab — Switch between open windows

Ctrl+T — New browser tab | Ctrl+W — Close current tab

Ctrl+Shift+T — Reopen last closed tab (lifesaver)

Ctrl+L — Jump to browser address bar

PrtScn or Cmd+Shift+4 — Screenshot

📁 File Management Basics

Organize your files NOW. Create a folder structure you can use for years:

📁 School → 📁 9th Grade → 📁 Math, 📁 English, 📁 Science...

📁 Personal → 📁 Resume, 📁 Photos, 📁 Projects

📁 Downloads — Clean this out monthly. It becomes a junk drawer fast.

File naming: Be specific. "Essay_Final.docx" tells you nothing in 6 months. Use: "English_Persuasive_Essay_Immigration_2025.docx"

Cloud backup: Use Google Drive, OneDrive, or iCloud. If your computer dies, your files survive. Save important school work to the cloud, not just your desktop.

File types: .docx (Word), .pdf (universal, can't edit), .xlsx (Excel), .pptx (PowerPoint), .jpg/.png (images), .mp4 (video), .zip (compressed folder)

📧 Professional Email

Email format for teachers, employers, colleges:

Subject line: Specific. "Question about Essay 2 — Period 3 English" not "hey" or "question"

Greeting: "Dear Mr./Ms. [Last Name]," or "Hello Professor [Name],"

Body: State who you are (name + class/context) in the first line. State your purpose in 2-3 sentences. Be polite and clear.

Closing: "Thank you," or "Best regards," then your full name.

Never: Use slang, emojis, or text abbreviations. Write in all caps. Forget to proofread. Reply-all unless necessary. Email when angry (save as draft, revisit tomorrow).

📊 Word, Excel & PowerPoint Basics

Microsoft Word / Google Docs:

Standard essay format: 12pt Times New Roman or Calibri, double-spaced, 1-inch margins. Always use header with your name, date, class.

Excel / Google Sheets:

Cells are referenced by column+row (A1, B3). Formulas start with =. Common: =SUM(A1:A10), =AVERAGE(B1:B10), =COUNT(), =IF(condition, true, false). Every job uses spreadsheets — learn the basics now.

PowerPoint / Google Slides:

Rule of thumb: 6 words per line, 6 lines per slide maximum. Images > text. Never read your slides — they are visual aids, not scripts. One idea per slide. Use consistent fonts and colors.

🔐 Online Safety & Digital Literacy

Passwords: Use a password manager (Bitwarden is free). Never reuse passwords across sites. Minimum 12 characters with letters, numbers, and symbols. Enable 2-factor authentication everywhere.

Phishing: Fake emails/texts that look real. Check the sender's actual email address (hover over it). Never click links in unexpected emails. Your bank will never ask for your password via email.

Public WiFi: Never log into banking, email, or sensitive accounts on public WiFi without a VPN. Anyone on the same network can potentially see your data.

AI tools: ChatGPT, Gemini, etc. are useful for brainstorming and explaining concepts. But submitting AI-generated work as your own is plagiarism. Schools are catching it. Use AI to learn, not to cheat.

🧠 How to Actually Study

Active recall (proven most effective): Close your notes. Try to write down everything you remember. Check what you missed. Repeat. This beats rereading by 3-4x.

Spaced repetition: Review material at increasing intervals: 1 day → 3 days → 7 days → 14 days. Apps like Anki automate this with flashcards.

Pomodoro Technique: 25 minutes focused study → 5 minute break → repeat 4x → longer break. Use the Study Timer in the School section.

Teach it: If you can explain a concept to someone else (or even to a stuffed animal), you understand it. If you get stuck explaining, that's where your gap is.

What does NOT work: Highlighting (gives false sense of learning). Rereading passively. Cramming the night before (your brain consolidates during sleep — study then sleep). Multitasking (your brain cannot focus on two things).

📝 Test-Taking Strategies

Before the test: Get 8 hours of sleep (more important than extra studying). Eat breakfast. Arrive early. Bring extra pencils/pens.

Multiple choice: Read the question BEFORE the answers. Cover the answers and answer in your head first. Eliminate obviously wrong answers — if stuck between two, go with your first instinct. "All of the above" and "None of the above" are often correct.

Essay tests: Read all questions first. Outline before writing (2-3 minutes). Start with the question you know best. Budget time per question. Leave 5 minutes to proofread.

When stuck: Skip it, come back later. Your subconscious processes it while you work on other questions. Never leave a multiple choice blank — guess if you must.

After the test: Review mistakes. Every wrong answer is a learning opportunity. Keep a "mistake log" to avoid repeating errors.

Learn to think like a programmer — logic, variables, loops, and functions.
🌐 Internet & Cybersecurity
Stay safe online. Learn how the internet works and how to protect yourself.

🚗
Driving
?HOW TO USE
Everything you need to know about getting your license, staying safe on the road, and taking care of your car. Read each card — this could save your life and your wallet.
🪪 Getting Your Learner's Permit

Age varies by state — most states issue learner's permits at 15 or 15½. Check your state's DMV website for exact requirements.

What you need: Proof of identity (birth certificate or passport), Social Security number, proof of residency (utility bill, school records), parent/guardian signature, application fee ($10-30 depending on state).

The written test covers road signs, right-of-way rules, speed limits, parking rules, and state-specific laws. Study the driver's manual — it's free on your state DMV website. Most states require 70-80% to pass. Take practice tests online — hundreds of free resources exist.

Restrictions: Most permits require a licensed adult (21+) in the passenger seat at all times. Many states restrict nighttime driving and number of passengers for permit holders.

🚗 Getting Your Full License

Practice hours matter. Most states require 40-70 hours of supervised driving before you can take the road test. Keep a log — your parent signs off on each session. Practice in different conditions: rain, night, highway, parking lots, busy intersections.

The driving test includes: Parallel parking, three-point turn, lane changes, stopping at intersections, obeying traffic signals, proper signaling, mirror checks, speed control, and general vehicle operation.

Common reasons people fail: Not checking mirrors enough, rolling through stop signs, going too far over/under the speed limit, incorrect lane positioning, improper following distance, and failure to yield.

Driver's Ed: Formal driver's education often reduces your insurance premium by 5-15%. Some states require it. Even if optional, it's worth taking — you learn defensive driving techniques that self-teaching misses.

📋 New Driver Checklist
⚠️ Distracted Driving — The #1 Killer of Teens

Car crashes are the leading cause of death for teens in America. And the primary cause is distracted driving — mostly phones. Reading a text at 55 mph means you travel the length of a football field with your eyes closed. It takes your brain 13 seconds to refocus after looking at a notification.

The rule: Phone goes in the glove box or backseat before the car moves. Not in your lap. Not face-down on the seat. Not "just for GPS" (mount it on the dash before driving). No exceptions. No text, call, or notification is worth your life or someone else's.

Other distractions: Eating while driving, adjusting music, talking to passengers, reaching for something. Each one takes your attention for seconds — and seconds kill at highway speeds.

🍺 Drunk & Impaired Driving

Zero tolerance. Under 21, the legal BAC limit in most states is .00-.02 — essentially zero. Any amount of alcohol impairs reaction time, judgment, and coordination. Even one drink affects your driving ability before you feel drunk.

This applies to weed too. THC impairs driving for hours after use. "I drive better high" is a myth — every study shows reduced reaction time and lane control.

Always have an exit plan: Ride-share app on your phone. A parent who will pick you up no questions asked (have this conversation in advance). A designated driver. Cash for a cab. Staying the night. Any of these is better than the alternative: a DUI costs $10,000+ in fines, license suspension, possible jail, and a criminal record that follows you. And that's if nobody gets hurt.

🛡️ Defensive Driving Essentials

Seatbelts — every time, every seat. Seatbelts reduce the risk of death by 45% and serious injury by 50%. They are the single most effective safety device in your car. Backseat passengers need them too — an unbelted backseat passenger becomes a projectile in a crash.

The 3-second following rule: Pick a fixed point ahead. When the car in front passes it, count "one-thousand-one, one-thousand-two, one-thousand-three." If you pass that point before you finish counting, you are too close. Double this distance in rain, triple in snow.

Scan constantly. Don't stare at the car directly in front of you. Your eyes should be scanning: far ahead for traffic flow, mirrors every 5-8 seconds, blind spots before lane changes. Anticipate what other drivers might do — assume they will do the dumbest possible thing.

🌧️ Weather & Night Driving

Rain: Most dangerous in the first 15 minutes — oil on the road mixes with water creating a slick surface. Slow down 5-10 mph. Headlights on. Increase following distance. If you hydroplane (feel the car floating), take your foot off the gas — don't brake hard.

Snow/ice: Reduce speed significantly. Brake gently and early. Steer into a skid (turn the wheel the direction your back end is sliding). Give yourself 3-4x normal following distance. Keep an emergency kit in the trunk: blanket, flashlight, water, snacks, phone charger.

Night: Your vision is reduced by 50%. Use high beams on dark rural roads but switch to low beams when a car approaches. Watch for animals. Clean your windshield — smudges create dangerous glare from oncoming headlights.

🛢️ Oil Changes — The Most Important Maintenance

Oil is your engine's blood. It lubricates hundreds of moving parts that would destroy each other without it. Running low or running old oil will kill your engine — a $3,000-8,000 repair vs. a $40-75 oil change.

How often: Every 5,000-7,500 miles for conventional oil, 7,500-10,000 for synthetic. Check your owner's manual for your car's specific recommendation. Synthetic costs more per change but lasts longer and protects better.

Check your oil monthly: Park on level ground. Wait 5 minutes after driving. Pull the dipstick, wipe it, reinsert fully, pull again. Oil should be between the two marks. If it's black and gritty, it's overdue for a change. If it's milky or foamy, there may be a serious problem — see a mechanic immediately.

🔧 Tire Care — Your Connection to the Road

Tire pressure: Check monthly with a $5 gauge. The correct pressure is on a sticker inside the driver's door jamb (NOT on the tire itself — that's the maximum). Underinflated tires waste gas, wear unevenly, and can blow out. Overinflated tires reduce grip.

Tread depth — the penny test: Insert a penny into the tread with Lincoln's head facing down. If you can see the top of his head, your tread is too low and you need new tires. Worn tires cannot grip in rain and are dangerous.

Tire rotation: Every 5,000-7,500 miles, rotate your tires so they wear evenly. Front tires wear faster because they steer. Most oil change shops will do this during your service. Budget $200-800 for a set of new tires when the time comes.

💡 Fluid Checks & Other Essentials

Coolant (antifreeze): Keeps your engine from overheating. Check the reservoir monthly — should be between min and max lines. Never open the radiator cap when the engine is hot.

Brake fluid: Should be clear to light gold. Dark or low brake fluid means your brakes need attention — don't delay this one.

Windshield washer fluid: Keep it full. Being unable to clean your windshield while driving is a real safety hazard. It costs $3 a gallon.

Lights: Walk around your car monthly and check all lights — headlights, tail lights, brake lights, turn signals. A burned-out light gets you pulled over and fined.

Air filter: Replace every 15,000-30,000 miles. A dirty air filter reduces fuel efficiency and engine performance. Costs $10-25 and takes 5 minutes to replace yourself.

🆘 What to Do in an Accident

1. Stop immediately. Leaving the scene is a crime (hit and run) even for minor fender benders. Pull to a safe location if possible — off the road.

2. Check for injuries. Call 911 if anyone is hurt. Don't move injured people unless there's immediate danger (fire, etc.).

3. Exchange information: Name, phone, insurance company, policy number, license plate, driver's license number. Take photos of both cars, the damage, the scene, and the other driver's info.

4. Document everything. Photos from multiple angles. Note the time, weather, road conditions. Get contact info from witnesses.

5. Do NOT admit fault. Don't say "I'm sorry" or "it was my fault." Just exchange information. Let the insurance companies and police determine fault.

6. File a police report even for minor accidents. Your insurance may require it, and it protects you legally.

7. Call your insurance company within 24 hours to report the accident. They will guide you through the claims process.

💰 The Real Cost of Driving

Owning a car costs more than the car payment. Most new drivers are shocked by the total. Here's the honest breakdown:

Insurance
$150-350/mo for teen drivers
Gas
$100-250/mo depending on commute
Maintenance
$100-150/mo average
Car Payment
$200-500/mo if financed
Registration
$50-500/yr by state
Total
$500-1,200/mo
🛡️ Insurance — What You Need to Know

Teen insurance is expensive because statistically you are the highest-risk drivers. But you can lower it:

• Good student discount (B average or above) — saves 5-15%

• Driver's Ed completion — saves 5-10%

• Stay on parents' policy — much cheaper than your own

• Drive a safe, modest car — insurance on a Honda Civic is half the cost of a BMW

• No tickets or accidents — one accident can double your rate for 3-5 years

Types of coverage: Liability (required — pays for damage you cause to others), Collision (pays for damage to your car), Comprehensive (theft, weather, animals), Uninsured Motorist (protects you if the other driver has no insurance). Higher deductibles = lower premiums but more out-of-pocket when you file a claim.

🏆
Sports
?HOW TO USE
Explore sports by level — middle school, high school, and college. Learn requirements, health tips, scholarship info, and what it takes to compete. Filter by Boys, Girls, or Both.
🎓 ATHLETIC SCHOLARSHIPS — WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW
Only about 2% of high school athletes receive athletic scholarships. NCAA Division I and II schools offer them, but Division III does not (they offer academic aid). To be recruited, start by the end of sophomore year: build a highlight reel, register with the NCAA Eligibility Center, email coaches directly, attend camps, and maintain your GPA. Minimum NCAA eligibility requires a 2.3 GPA in 16 core courses and specific SAT/ACT scores. Full-ride scholarships are rare outside football and basketball — most are partial scholarships that cover a percentage of tuition. The real value of college sports is the discipline, networking, and degree — not just the scholarship money.
⚠️ Important Disclaimer
The information in My Sports is provided for reference and personal tracking purposes only and does not constitute professional medical, athletic, or nutritional advice. YourLife.CC is not a substitute for qualified coaching, athletic training, or medical care. Injury prevention, nutrition, hydration, and conditioning information included here references publicly available guidance from reputable organizations (NATA, CDC, NIH, NSCA) and is intended as a starting point for your own research — not a complete or personalized protocol. Always consult a licensed medical professional before beginning any training program, making dietary changes, or managing an injury. YourLife.CC assumes no liability for injury, illness, or outcomes resulting from information tracked or referenced in this section. Your coach and doctor are your primary resources — use them.

🤝 Refer & Earn
Share YourLife CC with another family — you earn $10 cash, they get $10 off their first payment.
⚠️ IMPORTANT — PLEASE READ BEFORE SHARING
Who can earn referral rewards: Referral payouts are available only to adults 18 years of age or older. If the account holder is under 18, a parent or legal guardian must claim the payout on their behalf by contacting us with proof of relationship.

How payouts work: After your referred friend completes 30 days of an active paid subscription, we verify the referral and issue $10 via Venmo or PayPal to the registered adult on the 1st of the following month. Payouts to minors will not be processed — a parent or guardian must register a payout method.

One reward per household. Referral rewards cannot be earned by referring members of your own household or existing YourLife CC subscribers. We reserve the right to void referrals that appear fraudulent or self-referred.

By sharing your referral link you agree to these terms. Questions? Email us at
YOU EARN
$10
Venmo or PayPal · Adults 18+ only
FRIEND SAVES
$10
off their first payment
YOUR UNIQUE REFERRAL LINK
Loading your link...
Your link is unique to your account — every signup through it is tracked to you automatically.
💡 Tell your friend to use code:
REFER10
Entered at checkout · $10 off any plan
